plainMeaning: O Venerable one, I take up the vow of equanimity, give up all harmful activity in mind, speech and body for as long as I keep the deśāvagāśika, and I own up to my faults and let go of the wrong-doing self.
recitation: karemi bhante! sāmāiyaṁ, sāvajjaṁ jogaṁ paccakkhāmi, jāva desāvagāsiaṁ pajjuvāsāmi. duvihaṁ, tivihēṇaṁ. maṇeṇaṁ, vāyāe, kāeṇaṁ na karemi na kāravemi, tassa bhante! paḍikkamāmi, nindāmi, garihāmi appāṇaṁ vosirāmi.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. karemi < Skt. karomi, √kṛ 'to do' (1st sg. pres.)
wordByWord.notes: The performative verb that enacts the undertaking; recurs negated below as 'na karemi'.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. bhante < Skt. bhadanta / bhavant, voc. 'O reverend sir'
wordByWord.notes: First of two vocatives addressed to the guru/Tīrthaṅkara.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. < Skt. sāmāyika, from samāya 'coming together, equanimity'; acc. sg., object of karemi
Pkt. sāvajja < Skt. sāvadya (sa- + avadya 'blame'); acc. sg. qualifying jogaṁ
Pkt. joga < Skt. yoga 'application, activity'; acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: In Jain usage yoga = the threefold activity of mana/vacana/kāya that binds karma.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. < Skt. pratyākhyāmi, prati + ā + √khyā 'to declare against'; 1st sg. pres.
Pkt. jāva < Skt. yāvat 'as long as, until'; correlative of temporal duration
wordByWord.notes: Opens the duration clause that binds the vow to the observance.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. < Skt. deśāvakāśika, deśa 'region' + avakāśa 'scope/space'; acc. sg., object of pajjuvāsāmi
wordByWord.notes: Here this word substitutes for the ordinary sāmāyika's 'niyamaṁ', fixing the vow to the deśāvagāśika period.

wordByWord.gloss: I attend upon / I wait upon
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. < Skt. paryupāsāmi, pari + upa + √ās 'to sit close, serve'; 1st sg. pres.
wordByWord.notes: Connotes devoted, sustained attendance — here, keeping the observance for its set time.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. duviha < Skt. dvividha 'of two kinds'; adverbial acc.
wordByWord.notes: The two karaṇa: kṛta (oneself) and kārita (through another); the third, anumodita (approval), is deliberately not renounced here.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. tiviha < Skt. trividha 'of three kinds'; instr. sg. -eṇaṁ
wordByWord.notes: The three yoga listed next: mind, speech, body.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. maṇa < Skt. manas 'mind'; instr. sg.
Pkt. vāyā < Skt. vāc 'speech'; instr. sg.
Pkt. kāya < Skt. kāya 'body'; instr. sg.
Pkt./Skt. na, negative particle
wordByWord.notes: First of the paired negations governing the two karaṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. karemi < Skt. karomi, √kṛ; 1st sg. pres.
wordByWord.notes: Here negated: 'na karemi' = I will not do (myself) — the kṛta karaṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt./Skt. na, negative particle
Pkt. kāravemi < Skt. kārayāmi, causative of √kṛ; 1st sg. pres.
wordByWord.notes: 'na kāravemi' = I will not have (it) done by another — the kārita karaṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. tassa < Skt. tasya, gen. sg. of tad 'that'
wordByWord.notes: Refers back to any sāvadya-yoga already incurred; the genitive governs the confession verbs.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. bhante < Skt. bhadanta; voc.
wordByWord.notes: Second vocative, opening the confession before the guru.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. < Skt. pratikrāmāmi, prati + √kram 'to step back'; 1st sg. pres.
wordByWord.notes: First of the four self-correction verbs.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt./Skt. nindāmi, √nind 'to blame'; 1st sg. pres.
Pkt. garihāmi < Skt. garhāmi, √garh 'to reproach'; 1st sg. pres.
wordByWord.notes: Outward acknowledgement before the teacher; intensifies nindā.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. appāṇa < Skt. ātmānam, acc. sg. of ātman 'self'
Pkt. vosirāmi < Skt. vyutsṛjāmi, vi + ut + √sṛj 'to release, abandon'; 1st sg. pres.
wordByWord.notes: The final act of surrender; same verb that closes Iriyāvahiyaṁ and kāyotsarga formulae.

literalTranslation: I undertake, O Venerable one, the sāmāyika; I renounce sinful activity, for as long as I attend upon the deśāvagāśika. Two-fold, by three means — by mind, by speech, by body — I neither do nor cause to be done. For that, O Venerable one, I repent, I censure myself, I reproach myself; I cast off the self.
idiomaticTranslation: O Revered One, I take up the vow of equanimity and renounce all harmful activity for the whole span of my deśāvagāśika observance. In two ways and through three channels — mind, speech, and body — I will neither commit harm myself nor have it committed by another. And for whatever harm I have done, O Revered One, I turn back from it, I blame myself, I reproach myself before you, and I let go of the wrong-doing self.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Because the whole pratyākhyāna is this single verse, everything the practitioner needs is folded into one breath: undertaking, renunciation, scope, confession, surrender. The one word that earns its place here is the bound clause 'jāva desāvagāsiaṁ pajjuvāsāmi' — it tethers an ordinary sāmāyika to the tighter spatial-activity boundary of the tenth vow, so the equanimity is not free-floating but co-extensive with the shrunken field of movement the reciter has just accepted. The scope formula repays close attention: 'duvihaṁ tivihēṇaṁ' is two-fold by three-fold, not three-fold by three-fold. The reciter renounces only two of the three karaṇa — doing (kṛta, 'na karemi') and causing (kārita, 'na kāravemi') — across all three yoga of mind, speech, and body. The third karaṇa, anumodana (approval of another's act), is pointedly left unrenounced. This is the honest measure of a householder still enmeshed in family and commerce, who cannot truthfully vow to withhold all approval of worldly activity even for a fixed hour. The verse then pivots from prospective restraint to retrospective cleansing: 'tassa … paḍikkamāmi, nindāmi, garihāmi' graduates from turning-back, to inward self-blame, to open reproach before the guru, before the climactic 'appāṇaṁ vosirāmi' — the casting-off not of the soul but of the sin-stained, wrong-doing self. The two vocatives 'bhante' frame the verse like bookends: the first opens the vow, the second opens the confession, both keeping the guru as living witness.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the karaṇa-yoga matrix that underlies all Jain vrata-taking: three karaṇa (kṛta, kārita, anumodita) × three yoga (mana, vacana, kāya) yield nine possible degrees of renunciation, of which a full ascetic vow takes all nine ('tivihaṁ tivihēṇaṁ'). The lay deśāvagāśika sāmāyika here takes the two-by-three (six-fold) measure, a graded bhaṅga appropriate to the śrāvaka. The closing quartet — pratikramaṇa, nindā, garhā, vyutsarga — maps the standard ascending stages of ālocanā: withdrawal, self-reflection, confession-before-superior, and abandonment, the same movement that drives the larger pratikramaṇa ritual in miniature.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter today uses this verse to convert a vague intention to 'be good for an hour' into a precise, bounded commitment: I will not harm and will not have harm done, in thought, word, and deed, for exactly the span of this observance. The deliberately limited two-fold scope is itself a teaching in honest vow-taking — promise only what you can keep — while the four confession-verbs ensure the period closes in cleansing rather than self-congratulation.

notes: Spelling follows the book's orthography verbatim (e.g. પચ્ચખ્ખામિ, દેસાવગાસિઅં, તિવિહેણં); not normalized. The preface gives the scope clause as 'tivihaṇaṁ', whereas the verse text here reads તિવિહેણં (tivihēṇaṁ, instr.) — I have glossed the book's actual reading and flag the minor divergence. Note this lay form renounces only two karaṇa (kṛta, kārita) by three yoga; anumodita is intentionally omitted — a genuine doctrinal feature, not an OCR gap. Web references corroborate the standard sāmāyika formula but are not specific to the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) edition; exact mātrās/anusvāras should be taken from the source book as golden source. Per-token IAST follows standard Ardhamāgadhī conventions; vowel length in transliteration (e.g. -ēṇaṁ vs -eṇaṁ) reflects the book's reading where determinable.
